law: This sample shows how to use the SwiftUI Photos picker to browse and select a photo from your photo library.
The sample explores how to retrieve a SwiftUI image by using — a new SwiftUI protocol you use to move data.
Before you run the sample code project in Xcode, ensure you’re using iOS 16 or later, watchOS 9 or later, macOS 13 or later.
You must use a physical device when building the watchOS target in Xcode.
When handling many items at the same time, or large assets, use to load assets as files and reduce memory usage.
The sample app, PhotoBrowse, runs on iOS and tvOS and requires the following: iOS 12 or later.
After the grid view controller loads, it updates the image cache, which allows thumbnails to load quickly as the user scrolls: Implement the delegate method to use thumbnails instead of full assets.
However, it’s still good practice to write adjustment data so that potential future versions of the app—or other apps that understand your adjustment data format—could make use of it.

These changes may not necessarily occur inside your app’s functionality; they could originate from other apps, other devices, iCloud Photos, or Shared Albums: Be sure to unregister your change observer after the app’s main view controller goes away.
To use the extension, build and run the Slideshow Sample scheme in Xcode, which prompts you to open the macOS Photos app to use the extension.
When the project is complete, use the protocol method to unregister from change observation.
When assets are added or removed, the sample project calls to get an updated instance that you use to refresh your UI.
This sample shows how to use the Photos picker, and illustrates how to filter assets according to the user’s selection.
After you have a selection, you use an item provider to load the asset to display in the app.
Both and use the out-of-process Photos picker user interface.
A camera app, photo editing app, or library browsing app needs to use much more of ‘s functionality, but an app that’s only setting a basic profile photo doesn’t need photo library permission.
An app that only saves photos to the photo library can use the Add Photos Only permission level when requesting authorization.
Retrieving metadata for an asset doesn’t require special permission.
